questions.prompt: What's the cleanest distinction between an agent and an assistant?

choices.text: Agents are task-oriented — they run, complete a bounded task, and stop. Assistants are relationship-oriented — they maintain long-term memory, run on schedules/events, take initiative, and operate across many tasks in the user's day. An assistant's value compounds over time; an agent's value is task-by-task.
Assistants run in chat; agents run in workflows.

questions.explanation: Agents complete tasks; assistants maintain relationships. An agent that runs and ends, no matter how capable, isn't an assistant — even if you call it one. Assistants need long-term memory, schedule/event awareness, and a posture of initiative within trust boundaries.

questions.prompt: Your team built a great support chatbot. Marketing wants to call it 'your AI assistant.' Should you?

choices.text: No. The product is a chatbot (reactive Q&A in chat); users will expect assistant traits — memory, scheduled work, cross-tool initiative — and feel oversold when none exist. Two options: rename to 'AI support agent' to match reality, or invest in adding real assistant capability (memory, schedule, initiative) before claiming the title.
Call it an AI co-pilot to be safe.

questions.explanation: Unmet expectations are the fastest path to churn. Don't sell a chatbot as an assistant — users discover the gap fast. Either ship what you named or rename to what you shipped. Words shape user expectations; respect that.
